ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for deep-fried beef cubes with sweet and sour sauce; mapped to broader 'beef dish' class as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for deep-fried beef with sweet and sour sauce; A0CKP (Beef, cooked) used as the closest defensible broader anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific dish preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate serving of deep fried beef cubes with sweet and sour sauce is approximately 350 g, typical of a Zi Char portion shared at a table or served as a single-person main dish.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Beef, composite of trimmed retail cuts, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 1/8" fat, all grades, cooked (SR Legacy) combined with estimated sweet and sour sauce composition from USDA FoodData Central sauce components
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for deep-fried beef cubes with sweet and sour sauce as a composite dish. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked beef entries combined with estimated contributions from deep-frying (added fat from oil absorption) and sweet and sour sauce components (sugar, ketchup, vinegar, cornstarch). The input record values (energy 214 kcal, protein 12.86 g, fat 12.86 g, carbohydrate 12.86 g, sugar 7.14 g, sodium 342.86 mg, dietary fibre 0.86 g) are consistent with this composite estimation and are retained as the best available approximation. Singapore HPB Foodo database does not contain a direct entry for this dish. IMR Malaysia MyFCD does not contain a direct entry. USDA FoodData Central used as the closest approved secondary source for component-level validation.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Sweet and sour sauce / mixed beef dish analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for deep-fried beef cubes with sweet and sour sauce. GI estimated at 55 (low; GI ≤55 is classified as low per University of Sydney GI Database convention) based on the dominant carbohydrate contributors: cornstarch-battered coating (GI ~65 for cornstarch alone) and sweet and sour sauce containing sugar and ketchup, substantially attenuated by the high protein and fat content of the beef which blunts glycaemic response. A blended dish GI of approximately 55 is defensible for this mixed dish and falls at the upper boundary of the low GI category (≤55). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (55) × available carbohydrate per serving (45.0 g from 350 g × 12.86 g/100g) / 100 = 24.8. University of Sydney GI Database consulted for analogous food anchors.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 342.86 mg/100g (approximately 1200 mg per 350 g serving) combined with sugar from sweet and sour sauce creates an unfavourable cardiovascular load for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150 g or less, request the sweet and sour sauce served on the side to reduce sodium and sugar intake, and avoid pairing with other high-sodium Zi Char dishes in the same meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 7.14 g/100g and carbohydrate at 12.86 g/100g with low dietary fibre (0.86 g/100g) from the sweet and sour sauce and cornstarch coating produces a moderate glycaemic load of approximately 24.8 per standard serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should limit this dish to a half-plate portion (approximately 175 g), request reduced sauce or sauce on the side to lower sugar intake, and pair with non-starchy vegetables and a low-GI staple to moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 4.29 g/100g and trans fat at 0.14 g/100g from deep-frying, combined with cholesterol at 34.29 mg/100g and high sodium, present a moderate cardiovascular risk profile.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to occasional small portions, choose grilled or steamed beef preparations as a lower-risk alternative, and avoid repeated consumption of deep-fried dishes to manage saturated fat and trans fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that exacerbate joint inflammation; beef is high in arachidonic acid; bell peppers and tomato (ketchup) are nightshade vegetables.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish due to the combination of AGE-generating deep-frying, pro-inflammatory red meat, and nightshade ingredients; steamed or poached lean protein preparations are strongly preferred.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Deep Fried Beef Cubes with Sweet and Sour Sauce is a popular Zi Char dish served in Singaporean and Malaysian hawker centres and casual restaurants. The dish consists of bite-sized beef cubes that are coated in a seasoned cornstarch batter and deep-fried until golden and crispy, then tossed in a tangy-sweet sauce made from ketchup, vinegar, sugar, and soy sauce, typically garnished with bell peppers, pineapple chunks, and onion. The texture contrasts a crispy exterior with a tender beef interior, and the sauce delivers a characteristic sweet-sour flavour profile with mild umami depth. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 214 kcal, 12.86 g protein, 12.86 g fat (of which 4.29 g is saturated), 12.86 g carbohydrate, 7.14 g sugar, and 342.86 mg sodium. The high sugar content from the sauce and moderate sodium make this dish a moderate concern for individuals managing type 2 diabetes or hypertension. The deep-frying process generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), making it unsuitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis. A standard plate serving is approximately 350 g, yielding a glycaemic load of approximately 24.8. This dish is not vegetarian, not vegan, and halal status depends on the beef sourcing. It is a staple of Zi Char cuisine, which refers to the Chinese-style wok cooking tradition common in Singapore and Malaysia.

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA) contains a direct entry for deep-fried beef cubes with sweet and sour sauce as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central cooked beef entries combined with estimated sauce and frying contributions. The input record values from the GrabFood-sourced AI-generated record are consistent with this estimation and have been retained. Confidence is medium because the values are plausible and source-consistent but not directly verified against a single approved primary source entry for this exact dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite deep-fried beef dish with sweet and sour sauce are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central, Singapore HPB Foodo, or IMR Malaysia MyFCD as of 2024. The ethnic_tags field in the input contained 'Crab' which appears to be a data entry error for this beef dish and has been disregarded. The dish is classified as not halal-certified by default as halal status depends on beef sourcing and cannot be confirmed from available data. Trans fat at 0.14 g/100g is retained from the input record as a plausible value for a deep-fried preparation. All core macros (protein_g 12.86, fat_g 12.86, carbohydrates_g 12.86) are non-zero and reflect the composite nature of this dish with beef protein, frying oil fat, and sauce/batter carbohydrates. GI category corrected to 'low': per University of Sydney GI Database convention, GI ≤55 is classified as low; the estimated GI of 55 sits at the upper boundary of the low category.
